ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken burger / grilled chicken sandwich — composite bread-based dish with grilled chicken, vegetables and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers burger/sandwich composite dishes; used as closest defensible code for grilled chicken burger.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for grilled chicken burger as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard grilled chicken burger weighing approximately 180 g, comprising a toasted bun, grilled chicken patty or fillet, lettuce, tomato slices, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Chicken Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Grilled Chicken Burger (F05020050) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled chicken burger as a composite dish. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the GI of a white-bread hamburger bun (GI ~61–72, mean ~66) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing component. Glycemic load per 180 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (66 × 27.18) / 100 ≈ 17.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item due to its high sodium content of 470 mg/100 g; request no added sauces or salt, and choose a wholemeal bun to partially offset the sodium load with additional potassium and fibre.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (15.1 g/100 g) with medium GI (~66) and low sugar (3 g/100 g); high protein (14.2 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume this item in controlled portions (one burger, ~180 g) as the high protein content moderates postprandial glucose response; choose a wholemeal bun to lower the glycaemic index and avoid sugary condiments such as ketchup or BBQ sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 470 mg/100 g is elevated; total fat is very low at 0.09 g/100 g and the grilled preparation avoids added saturated or trans fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very low fat content and grilled preparation make this a heart-friendly protein source, but the high sodium content (470 mg/100 g) is a concern for cardiovascular disease management; request no added salt or high-sodium condiments and limit portion size to one burger.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Grilled lean chicken provides no significant arachidonic acid load; tomato is a nightshade vegetable with potential individual sensitivity in some RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled chicken is a lean anti-inflammatory protein choice for RA patients; however, tomato as a nightshade vegetable may trigger individual sensitivity in some patients — monitor personal response and avoid deep-fried variants or high-sodium condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A grilled chicken burger is a Western-style bread-based sandwich consisting of a grilled chicken fillet or patty served in a toasted bun with lettuce, tomato slices, and sauce. The chicken is cooked by grilling rather than frying, resulting in a lean, low-fat protein source with a lightly charred, tender texture. Per 100 g, the burger provides 118 kcal, 14.2 g protein, 0.09 g fat, and 15.1 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-fat, moderate-carbohydrate food. Sodium is elevated at 470 mg/100 g, primarily from the sauce and seasoning. The item is suitable for halal diets and is commonly consumed as a quick-service or fast-food meal in Singapore and internationally. Clinically, the high protein and low fat profile are favourable for weight management and cardiovascular health, but the sodium content warrants caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycaemic index (~66) and moderate carbohydrate load require portion control for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. Calcium is present at 58 mg/100 g and iron at 0.84 mg/100 g, contributing modestly to micronutrient intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a grilled chicken burger?
Is grilled chicken burger suitable for diabetics?
Is grilled chicken burger high in sodium?
Can I eat grilled chicken burger if I have hypertension?
What is the protein content of a grilled chicken burger?
Is grilled chicken burger good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05020050) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate component (white burger bun) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reduces overall confidence for the GI component to medium; however, the nutrient profile confidence remains high.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite grilled chicken burger are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020050 as of 2026-04-16. fat_g is 0.09 g/100 g (not zero); this is a confirmed very low but non-zero value from the HPB source reflecting the lean grilled chicken and minimal sauce fat content — not a zero-macro situation. This is a Western-cuisine food item; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean-origin dish and no established local-language names exist for this generic Western fast-food item. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on the GI of a white hamburger bun from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for a composite grilled chicken burger was found in approved sources.
